The planned institution, Foris Dax National Trust Bank — operating as Crypto.com National Trust Bank — would function as a limited-purpose trust bank focused on digital asset custody, staking, and trade settlement.

The charter does not permit the bank to accept deposits or issue loans. Instead, it consolidates Crypto.com's institutional custody offerings under a single federal umbrella, a significant upgrade from its current state-level arrangement through Crypto.com Custody Trust Co., which is regulated by New Hampshire's banking department. For ETF issuers, asset managers, and other institutional clients, a nationally chartered custodian simplifies compliance and eliminates the patchwork of state-by-state regulatory requirements that has long complicated digital asset custody.

Crypto.com filed its application with the OCC in October and joins a rapidly expanding cohort of crypto-native firms pursuing federal trust charters. In December, BitGo, Circle, Ripple, Paxos, and Fidelity Digital Assets all received similar conditional approvals. Just last week, Stripe's stablecoin subsidiary Bridge secured its own initial nod for a national trust bank. The clustering of these approvals suggests the OCC has adopted a more welcoming posture toward crypto custody under federal supervision — a marked shift from the regulatory ambiguity that characterized earlier years.

The conditional approval, while not final, represents a meaningful milestone. The bank would also support services on Crypto.com's proprietary Cronos blockchain, potentially giving the exchange a competitive edge in offering vertically integrated custody and settlement infrastructure. The move arrives at a turbulent moment for crypto markets, with Bitcoin ETFs experiencing a historic $3.8 billion outflow streak over five weeks and Bitcoin trading near $63,980.

The broader trend of crypto firms embedding themselves within federal regulatory structures signals a maturing industry increasingly oriented toward institutional capital. Whether these charter approvals translate into durable competitive advantages will depend on how quickly the firms can meet the OCC's remaining conditions and begin operations.

articles.headline: Chinese Hackers Exploited Ivanti VPN Backdoor to Breach 119 Organizations
articles.subheadline: Bloomberg investigation reveals how private equity cost-cutting weakened cybersecurity at the enterprise software giant
articles.body:
Chinese state-linked hackers planted a secret backdoor in Pulse Secure VPN software and used it to compromise 119 organizations, including European and U.S. military contractors, according to a Bloomberg investigation published Sunday. The breach, which Ivanti discovered in February 2021 after acquiring Pulse Secure, had not been previously reported at this scale.

The hackers exploited the hidden backdoor to gain access to dozens of companies and government agencies that relied on Pulse Secure's widely deployed VPN appliances for secure remote access. Cybersecurity firm Mandiant was reportedly aware of the intrusions and alerted Ivanti that threat actors had used the vulnerability to penetrate defense industry networks on both sides of the Atlantic. Neither Ivanti nor Mandiant responded to requests for comment.

The Bloomberg report draws a direct line between the breach's severity and the cost-cutting measures imposed after private equity firm Clearlake Capital Group acquired Ivanti in 2017. Particularly damaging were rounds of layoffs in 2022 that eliminated employees with deep institutional knowledge of the company's products and their security architectures. The pattern mirrors what happened at Citrix, a rival remote-access provider that experienced similar cybersecurity deterioration after Elliott Investment Management and Vista Equity Partners took the company private in a 2022 deal.

The Pulse Secure incident proved to be a harbinger. Ivanti's VPN products have been at the center of at least two additional major cyberattacks since. In early 2024, the U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency took the extraordinary step of ordering all federal agencies to disconnect their Ivanti VPN appliances within 48 hours after discovering that hackers were actively exploiting zero-day vulnerabilities unknown even to the vendor. In 2025, Ivanti again warned customers that attackers were leveraging a critical flaw in its Connect Secure product to breach corporate networks.

The revelations raise uncomfortable questions about the role of private equity in enterprise software security. As firms acquire technology companies and pursue aggressive cost reduction, the resulting erosion of engineering talent and security expertise can create vulnerabilities that ripple across thousands of downstream customers — including those in the most sensitive corners of government and defense. With VPN appliances serving as the front door to corporate and government networks, the stakes of underinvestment in their security could hardly be higher.

articles.headline: Stocks Slide as AI Profit Fears and Tariff Uncertainty Rattle Markets
articles.subheadline: Investors flee to bonds and gold as dual anxieties over artificial intelligence returns and trade policy erode risk appetite
articles.body:
Global equity markets sold off sharply as investors grappled with a confluence of concerns — mounting doubts about whether artificial intelligence investments will translate into corporate profits and persistent uncertainty surrounding U.S. tariff policy. The risk-off mood sent bonds higher, pushed Bitcoin below $65,000, and drove gold prices to fresh gains.

The sell-off reflects a growing unease among market participants who had until recently been willing to pay steep premiums for AI-linked stocks. After quarters of heavy capital expenditure by major technology firms building out data centers and AI infrastructure, investors are increasingly demanding evidence that these outlays will generate meaningful returns. That skepticism, layered atop unresolved questions about the scope and timing of new trade levies, created a potent cocktail of anxiety that rippled across asset classes.

Fixed-income markets were the primary beneficiary of the flight from risk, with bond prices climbing as yields fell. The move suggests traders are positioning for a potential slowdown in economic activity should tariffs materialize at the levels some policymakers have signaled. Gold, long a haven during periods of geopolitical and economic stress, also attracted fresh buying interest as investors sought shelter from the equity downdraft.

Cryptocurrency markets offered no refuge. Bitcoin's decline below the $65,000 mark underscored the broader retreat from speculative assets, a reversal from periods when digital currencies occasionally traded as uncorrelated alternatives during traditional market turbulence. The drop suggests that in the current environment, crypto is behaving more like a high-beta risk asset than a store of value.

The simultaneous pressure from AI skepticism and trade policy ambiguity presents a challenging backdrop for portfolio managers heading deeper into 2026. With corporate earnings season set to provide fresh data on whether AI spending is beginning to pay off, and trade negotiations remaining fluid, market volatility appears likely to persist. Investors will be watching closely for any concrete signals — either from boardrooms or from Washington — that could tip sentiment decisively in one direction.

articles.headline: OpenAI Enlists Four Consulting Giants to Accelerate Enterprise AI Adoption
articles.subheadline: The 'Frontier Alliance' with BCG, McKinsey, Accenture, and Capgemini marks a strategic shift in how OpenAI sells to large companies
articles.body:
OpenAI is turning to the world's most influential consulting firms to crack the enterprise market, announcing multi-year partnerships with Boston Consulting Group, McKinsey, Accenture, and Capgemini under a new initiative called the "Frontier Alliance."

The move, revealed Monday, represents a significant strategic pivot for the AI company. Rather than relying solely on direct sales, OpenAI is betting that consultants — who already hold deep relationships with Fortune 500 companies and specialize in organizational transformation — can drive adoption of its enterprise products in ways that a technology vendor alone cannot. OpenAI's Forward Deployed Engineering team will work alongside the four firms to integrate its enterprise-focused tools, particularly the recently launched OpenAI Frontier platform, into clients' technology stacks. Frontier, a no-code platform introduced in early February, allows organizations to build, deploy, and manage AI agents using OpenAI's models.

The alliance tacitly acknowledges a stubborn reality: enterprise adoption of AI has been sluggish, with many large organizations struggling to find meaningful return on investment from their AI initiatives. BCG CEO Christoph Schweizer framed the challenge in OpenAI's announcement, noting that "AI alone does not drive transformation" and must be embedded in redesigned processes and organizational culture to deliver results. The consulting approach aims to go beyond bolting AI onto existing workflows, instead persuading companies to fundamentally restructure their operations around AI capabilities.

OpenAI is not alone in courting the consulting world. Rival Anthropic has recently signed its own deals with Deloitte and Accenture, signaling that the battle for enterprise AI dominance is increasingly being fought through intermediaries rather than direct sales alone. Notably, Accenture now holds partnerships with both competitors, positioning itself at the center of the emerging enterprise AI ecosystem.

The Frontier Alliance builds on a broader enterprise offensive OpenAI has mounted in 2026. CFO Sarah Friar identified enterprise as a major priority in a January blog post, and the company has since struck sizable deals with Snowflake and ServiceNow. OpenAI also appointed Barret Zoph to lead its enterprise sales efforts in January, further underscoring the company's determination to convert its consumer AI dominance into corporate revenue. With annual revenue targets climbing and investor expectations intensifying, success in the enterprise market — where contracts tend to be larger and more durable — could prove decisive for OpenAI's long-term financial trajectory.

articles.headline: PayPal Draws Takeover Interest as Prolonged Stock Slump Opens Door
articles.subheadline: Shares surge nearly 10% on reports that potential acquirers are circling the digital payments giant after steep valuation decline
articles.body:
PayPal Holdings Inc. has attracted acquisition interest from potential buyers looking to capitalize on a dramatic decline in the company's market value, according to people with knowledge of the matter. The stock jumped as much as 9.7% on the news, offering shareholders a rare jolt of upward momentum after a prolonged downturn.

The digital payments company, once a Wall Street darling that commanded a market capitalization north of $350 billion at its 2021 peak, has seen its stock lose roughly half its value in a punishing slide that has left it trading at a fraction of its former highs. That steep discount appears to have caught the attention of suitors who see an opportunity to acquire a company that still processes hundreds of billions of dollars in payment volume annually and operates one of the most recognized brands in fintech.

The identity of the interested parties was not disclosed, and it remains unclear whether any formal approach has been made or whether discussions could lead to a definitive offer. Takeover speculation involving companies of PayPal's scale often fails to materialize into completed transactions, particularly given the regulatory scrutiny that would accompany a deal of this magnitude. PayPal's enterprise value, even after its decline, would make any acquisition one of the largest in the financial technology sector's history.

PayPal has faced mounting competitive pressure in recent years from rivals including Apple Pay, Block's Cash App, and a wave of buy-now-pay-later services that have eroded its dominance in online checkout. The company has cycled through strategic pivots under successive leadership teams, attempting to reignite growth while managing investor expectations. A potential acquirer — whether a major technology platform, a large financial institution, or a private equity consortium — would be betting that PayPal's vast user base and global payments infrastructure remain undervalued relative to their long-term earnings potential.

The surge in PayPal's share price following the report underscores how hungry investors have become for a catalyst after years of underperformance. Whether the takeover interest evolves into a concrete bid or fades as due diligence reveals the complexity of the business, the development signals that the market views PayPal's current valuation as disconnected from its strategic assets — a gap that dealmakers appear eager to exploit.

articles.headline: Novo Nordisk's Next-Gen Obesity Drug Falls Short of Eli Lilly Rival
articles.subheadline: CagriSema delivered 20.2% weight loss versus 23.6% for Lilly's tirzepatide, deepening Novo's competitive woes
articles.body:
Novo Nordisk's much-anticipated next-generation obesity treatment CagriSema failed to match the weight-loss efficacy of Eli Lilly's rival drug tirzepatide in clinical trials, dealing another setback to the Danish pharmaceutical giant as it struggles to defend its position in the booming weight-loss market.

In an 84-week trial, patients receiving a standard dose of CagriSema achieved 20.2% body weight reduction, falling meaningfully short of the 23.6% weight loss delivered by Lilly's tirzepatide — a gap of 3.4 percentage points that could have significant commercial implications. The results represent a fresh blow to Novo Nordisk, which had pinned substantial hopes on CagriSema as the compound that would help it recapture momentum against an increasingly dominant Eli Lilly.

The head-to-head comparison underscores the competitive dynamics reshaping the global obesity drug market, which analysts project could exceed $100 billion in annual sales by the end of the decade. Novo Nordisk pioneered the modern obesity treatment era with semaglutide, marketed as Wegovy, but Eli Lilly's tirzepatide — sold as Zepbound for weight management — has steadily gained ground with superior efficacy data. CagriSema, which combines semaglutide with the amylin analog cagrilintide, was designed to leapfrog the competition by targeting multiple biological pathways simultaneously.

The disappointing results raise difficult strategic questions for Novo Nordisk. The company must now decide how aggressively to pursue regulatory approval and commercialization of CagriSema when the data suggest it may enter the market as the second-best option rather than the category leader. Investors have already punished Novo Nordisk shares over the past year as confidence in its obesity pipeline has wavered, and the latest trial data is unlikely to reverse that sentiment.

The setback comes as the broader pharmaceutical industry faces a period of upheaval. Merck, grappling with its own strategic challenges as patent protection for its blockbuster cancer immunotherapy Keytruda approaches expiration, announced plans to split its main pharmaceutical unit into two divisions in an effort to spotlight faster-growing segments of the business. Together, the developments illustrate how even the industry's largest players are under intense pressure to innovate and restructure as competitive and patent landscapes shift beneath them.

articles.headline: 25 Mexican National Guards Killed in Cartel Violence After El Mencho's Death
articles.subheadline: Mexico deploys 2,500 troops as Jalisco New Generation Cartel unleashes attacks across 20 states following drug lord's killing
articles.body:
At least 25 members of Mexico's National Guard have been killed in a sweeping wave of cartel violence that erupted across the country after the death of Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, the feared drug lord known as "El Mencho," Mexico's security ministry confirmed Monday.

El Mencho, who led the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG) and was Mexico's most wanted fugitive, died Sunday after being captured by Mexican special forces in the town of Tapalpa, Jalisco state. According to Defense Secretary Ricardo Trevilla, military commandos tracked him through a romantic partner he was meeting. A firefight between his bodyguards and the commandos left El Mencho seriously wounded, and he died while being transported to Mexico City. Six of his security guards were killed and three soldiers were injured in the operation.

The reprisals were immediate and devastating. Members of the CJNG launched coordinated attacks across at least 20 Mexican states, blocking roads with spikes and nails, commandeering and torching buses, and setting fire to banks and businesses. In Puerto Vallarta and other cities in Jalisco, smoke billowed from burning vehicles as streets emptied and authorities ordered residents to shelter in their homes. Security Secretary Omar Garcia Harfuch said a prison guard, a state prosecutor's office employee, and 30 members of El Mencho's criminal organization were also killed in the unrest.

Mexico responded by deploying 2,500 soldiers to the western part of the country. President Claudia Sheinbaum praised the military operation and sought to project stability, declaring Monday morning that roadblocks had been cleared. "There is calm, there is government, there are armed forces and there is a lot of coordination," she said. Mexico confirmed that while U.S. intelligence provided "complementary information" that aided the operation, no American forces participated directly in the raid.

The U.S. State Department had offered a $15 million reward for information leading to El Mencho's capture. His death eliminates the leader of one of the most powerful and violent drug trafficking organizations in the Western Hemisphere, but the immediate aftermath underscores the destabilizing risks that accompany the removal of a cartel figurehead. Whether the CJNG fragments into competing factions or consolidates under new leadership will be a critical question for Mexican security forces in the weeks ahead.

articles.headline: Pentagon Threatens Anthropic With 'Supply Chain Risk' Label Over AI Limits
articles.subheadline: Defense Secretary Hegseth summons CEO Dario Amodei to discuss military use of Claude after the company refused surveillance and autonomous weapons requests
articles.body:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has summoned Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei to the Pentagon for a Tuesday meeting that could determine the future of one of the most consequential partnerships between the U.S. military and the AI industry. At issue: Anthropic's refusal to allow its Claude AI system to be used for mass domestic surveillance and the development of weapons capable of firing without human oversight.

The confrontation marks a dramatic escalation in tensions between the Department of Defense and one of the world's leading AI companies. According to Axios, the Pentagon is threatening to designate Anthropic a "supply chain risk" — a classification normally reserved for foreign adversaries such as Chinese tech firms — if the company does not relent on its usage restrictions. Such a designation would void Anthropic's existing $200 million contract with the DOD, signed last summer, and compel other Pentagon partners to sever ties with Claude entirely.

The friction between Anthropic and the military became public following the January 3 special operations raid that led to the capture of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro, during which Claude was reportedly deployed in a supporting role. While the details of that use remain classified, the operation apparently exposed fundamental disagreements over where Anthropic draws ethical lines around its technology. A source familiar with the matter told Axios that Hegseth intends to present Amodei with an ultimatum: cooperate fully with the Pentagon's demands or face expulsion from the defense ecosystem.

The standoff poses significant risks for both sides. For Anthropic, losing its defense contract and being blacklisted across the Pentagon's supply chain would represent a major financial and reputational blow. For the Department of Defense, replacing Anthropic's technology would be a substantial undertaking, given Claude's integration into military workflows and the limited number of AI systems with comparable capabilities. The situation also raises broader questions about the degree to which AI companies can maintain independent ethical policies when contracting with the federal government.

The outcome of Tuesday's meeting could set a precedent for the entire AI industry's relationship with the U.S. military. Other leading AI firms, including OpenAI and Google, have faced their own internal debates over defense work. If the Pentagon successfully pressures Anthropic into dropping its restrictions — or follows through on its threat to sideline the company — it will send a clear signal about the government's willingness to use its contracting power to override corporate AI safety policies.

articles.headline: Zelensky Declares Putin Has Already Started World War Three
articles.subheadline: In a BBC interview from Kyiv, Ukraine's president rejects territorial concessions and vows eventual restoration of 1991 borders
articles.body:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has declared that Russian President Vladimir Putin has already initiated a third world war, warning that only sustained military and economic pressure can halt Moscow's ambitions. In a wide-ranging interview with the BBC conducted at the heavily fortified government headquarters in Kyiv, Zelensky rejected the territorial concessions that both Russia and, implicitly, the Trump administration have signaled as prerequisites for a ceasefire.

"I believe that Putin has already started it," Zelensky told BBC international editor Jeremy Bowen. "The question is how much territory he will be able to seize and how to stop him." Asked whether surrendering the roughly 20 percent of the Donetsk region still under Ukrainian control — along with additional land in Kherson and Zaporizhzhia — might be a reasonable price for peace, Zelensky was unequivocal. "I don't look at it simply as land. I see it as abandonment — weakening our positions, abandoning hundreds of thousands of our people who live there," he said, adding that any such withdrawal would fracture Ukrainian society.

Zelensky acknowledged that a territorial deal might temporarily satisfy Putin but argued the Russian leader would need only two to three years to regroup before launching further aggression — a timeline shorter than the three-to-five-year estimate offered by some European partners. He maintained that restoring Ukraine's 1991 borders remains the ultimate goal, though he conceded it is not achievable in the near term given Russia's military size and Ukraine's limited weapons supply. "What is land without people? Honestly, nothing," he said.

The interview comes amid intensifying diplomatic pressure from Washington. President Donald Trump has urged Ukraine to "come to the table fast" ahead of ceasefire negotiations in Geneva and has halted nearly all direct military aid shipments, though U.S. intelligence sharing continues and European allies are spending billions purchasing American weapons to funnel to Kyiv. Zelensky, who reportedly has been coached by British National Security Adviser Jonathan Powell to avoid public clashes with Trump, sidestepped direct criticism of the American president but made clear that any security guarantees must be institutionalized through Congressional approval rather than resting on a single leader's word. "Presidents change, but institutions stay," he said.

On Trump's demand that Ukraine hold elections by summer — echoing a Russian talking point questioning Zelensky's legitimacy — the Ukrainian leader said he was open to the idea in principle but cited enormous logistical barriers, including millions of displaced refugees abroad and Russian-occupied territory. He said he had not decided whether to seek reelection. Amid the geopolitical uncertainty, Zelensky projected characteristic defiance. When asked whether Ukraine could lose the war, he responded by noting that his interviewer was sitting in Kyiv, in the capital of an independent nation. "Will we lose? Of course not, because we are fighting for Ukraine's independence."

articles.headline: Ferrofluid Simulations Show Tunable Heat Transfer in Bend Channels
articles.subheadline: Researchers model how external magnetic fields can dynamically control cooling performance in channel flows
articles.body:
A new computational study demonstrates that ferrofluids — liquids containing suspended magnetic nanoparticles — can be harnessed to significantly enhance and precisely tune heat transfer in bent channel geometries through the application of external magnetic fields.

Researchers Nadish Anand and Warren Jasper published the first part of a two-part investigation on the arXiv preprint server, presenting numerical CFD (computational fluid dynamics) simulations of ferrohydrodynamic flows through a 90-degree bend channel. The study systematically varies the parameters of non-uniform magnetic fields applied to the flowing ferrofluid, characterizing how different field configurations alter thermal transport properties. The work sits at the intersection of applied physics and chemical physics, addressing a longstanding engineering challenge: how to create cooling systems whose performance can be adjusted on demand without changing the hardware.

Conventional liquid cooling systems rely on fixed geometries and constant fluid properties, limiting their adaptability. Ferrofluids offer a compelling alternative because their flow behavior responds directly to magnetic fields, allowing engineers to modify velocity profiles, mixing patterns, and secondary flows simply by adjusting an electromagnet. In a bend channel specifically, the interplay between centrifugal forces at the turn and magnetically induced body forces creates complex flow structures that can either suppress or dramatically amplify heat transfer from the channel walls. By mapping these interactions across multiple parameters, the researchers aim to provide a design framework for systems that can dynamically respond to changing thermal loads.

The practical implications span several industries where thermal management is critical. Electronics cooling, where heat loads fluctuate with processor demand, stands to benefit from a system that can ramp cooling intensity up or down magnetically. Similarly, chemical processing and energy systems that encounter variable operating conditions could leverage ferrofluid-based heat exchangers for more efficient and responsive thermal control.

Designated as Part 1 of the study, this paper focuses on establishing the numerical modeling methodology and characterizing the fundamental physics at play. A subsequent installment is expected to build on these results, potentially with experimental validation or optimization studies. The research underscores growing interest in magnetically responsive fluids as a platform for next-generation thermal management, where tunability and active control replace the static designs that have dominated heat exchanger engineering for decades.

articles.headline: Tiny Reef Fish Use Tools to Test Mirrors, Suggesting Wider Self-Awareness
articles.subheadline: Cleaner wrasse dropped food near mirrors and tracked its reflection — behavior previously seen only in dolphins and manta rays
articles.body:
Cleaner wrasse, small fish that inhabit coral reefs, have demonstrated a startling capacity for self-awareness by not only recognizing their own reflections but deliberately using objects to probe how mirrors work, according to new research from Osaka Metropolitan University published in Scientific Reports.

The study, led by Shumpei Sogawa and Masanori Kohda at the university's Graduate School of Science, found that when marked with spots resembling parasites, cleaner wrasse (Labroides dimidiatus) attempted to scrub off the marks after seeing themselves in a mirror — in some cases within the first hour. The average response time was just 82 minutes, a dramatic improvement over the four to six days observed in prior mirror experiments with the species. The difference, the researchers believe, came from reversing the usual protocol: fish were marked before seeing a mirror for the first time, meaning the reflection immediately confirmed a sensation they already felt on their bodies.

More remarkable still was what happened after several days of mirror exposure. Some fish picked up small pieces of shrimp from the tank floor, carried them upward, and released them near the mirror's surface. They then tracked the food's descent in the reflection while repeatedly touching the glass — a behavior the researchers classify as "contingency testing." Rather than using their own bodies to explore the mirror, the wrasse employed an external object to study how reflected space corresponded to reality. Until now, this kind of exploratory mirror behavior had been documented almost exclusively in cognitively advanced marine animals such as dolphins and manta rays, which blow bubbles and observe them in reflections.

The findings carry significant implications for how scientists understand the distribution of self-awareness across the animal kingdom. The mirror test, first developed in 1970, has long served as a benchmark for self-recognition, with only a handful of species — great apes, elephants, magpies, and certain cetaceans — consistently passing it. The cleaner wrasse's performance challenges the assumption that such cognition is confined to large-brained mammals and birds. "Self-awareness may not have evolved only in the limited number of species that passed the mirror test but may be more widely prevalent across a broader range of taxonomic groups, including fish," Sogawa said.

Kohda suggested the work could reverberate beyond evolutionary biology, potentially influencing animal welfare policy, medical research ethics, and even artificial intelligence development. The team plans to extend its investigations to other species, including invertebrates, as part of a broader effort to map the cognitive landscape of the animal world.

articles.headline: Supreme Court to Hear Oil Industry Appeal on Climate Lawsuits
articles.subheadline: Exxon Mobil and Suncor Energy win high court review in bid to block dozens of state and city climate suits
articles.body:
The U.S. Supreme Court has agreed to take up an appeal by Exxon Mobil Corp. and Suncor Energy Inc., giving the oil industry a high-profile opportunity to shut down a growing wave of climate change lawsuits filed by cities and states across the country. The decision to grant certiorari, announced on February 23, sets the stage for one of the most consequential environmental legal battles to reach the nation's highest court in years.

The cases at the center of the dispute involve dozens of lawsuits brought by municipalities and state governments alleging that major fossil fuel companies bear responsibility for the damages caused by climate change. Plaintiffs have argued that oil producers knew for decades that their products were contributing to global warming and deliberately misled the public, drawing parallels to the litigation strategies that ultimately forced the tobacco industry into massive settlements. The suits have sought billions of dollars in damages to cover costs ranging from infrastructure repairs to public health expenditures linked to rising temperatures and extreme weather events.

For the oil industry, the Supreme Court hearing represents a critical chance to resolve a threshold legal question that has largely gone against them in lower courts: whether these cases belong in federal court, where companies believe they stand a better chance of dismissal, or in state courts, where plaintiffs have preferred to litigate. Federal judges have repeatedly sent the cases back to state courts, and appellate courts have largely upheld those rulings. The Supreme Court's willingness to intervene signals that at least four justices found the industry's arguments worthy of full consideration.

The implications extend well beyond Exxon and Suncor. A ruling favorable to the oil companies could effectively neutralize climate litigation strategies that environmental advocates and plaintiff attorneys have spent years developing. Conversely, a decision affirming the lower courts would allow the suits to proceed in state venues across the country, potentially exposing the industry to a patchwork of costly trials and settlements.

Legal observers expect oral arguments later this term, with a decision likely by the summer of 2026. The outcome could reshape the legal landscape around corporate accountability for climate change and influence how governments, investors, and fossil fuel companies approach the energy transition for years to come.

articles.headline: Russian-Linked Crypto Exchanges Still Evading Western Sanctions, Elliptic Warns
articles.subheadline: Blockchain analytics firm identifies platforms processing billions in crypto tied to sanctioned entities despite regulatory crackdowns
articles.body:
Blockchain analytics firm Elliptic has identified several Russian-linked cryptocurrency exchanges that continue to facilitate transactions for sanctioned entities, processing billions of dollars in digital assets that circumvent Western financial restrictions imposed after Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine.

The report, published Friday, names platforms including Bitpapa, ABCeX, Rapira, and Aifory Pro as key nodes in a financial network that converts rubles into cryptocurrency, moves funds across borders outside traditional banking channels, and enables cash-outs through overseas brokers. Among the most notable findings, ABCeX — which operates from Moscow's Federation Tower — has processed at least $11 billion in crypto transactions, with flows directed to sanctioned exchanges such as Garantex and Aifory Pro. Bitpapa, a UAE-registered peer-to-peer platform sanctioned by the U.S. Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control in March 2024, reportedly sent roughly 9.7% of its outgoing crypto to sanctioned entities and allegedly rotates wallet addresses to hinder transaction tracing. Rapira, another exchange flagged in the report, processed more than $72 million in transactions with sanctioned exchange Grinex.

The findings arrive amid mounting evidence that cryptocurrency infrastructure has become a critical tool for sanctions evasion at scale. A separate Elliptic report from last month revealed that transactions involving the ruble-pegged stablecoin A7A5 have surpassed $100 billion, while Tether's USDT has emerged as a key asset for Russian actors seeking to sidestep Western restrictions. A complementary study from rival analytics firm TRM Labs showed that illicit entities received $141 billion in stablecoins in 2025 — a five-year high — with sanctions-related activity accounting for 86% of illicit crypto flows. More than half of that volume was linked to the A7A5 token, whose Russian executives dispute allegations that their operations are unlawful.

The scale of the problem underscores the challenge facing Western governments, which have frozen substantial Russian assets since the full-scale invasion — roughly $250 billion by the European Union and nearly $35 billion by the United Kingdom. Yet the crypto ecosystem continues to offer pathways that reduce reliance on the conventional financial system and complicate enforcement. Aifory Pro, for instance, offers cash-to-crypto services across Moscow, Dubai, and Türkiye, illustrating how these networks span multiple jurisdictions.

For regulators, the Elliptic report adds urgency to calls for tighter oversight of crypto platforms operating in gray-zone jurisdictions. As stablecoin markets grow and cross-border crypto infrastructure matures, the gap between sanctions policy and enforcement capacity appears to be widening — with billions of dollars flowing through channels that traditional financial controls were never designed to reach.

articles.headline: Massive Storm Batters US East Coast, Shutting Down NYC Tri-State Travel
articles.subheadline: Over 10,000 flights canceled and thousands left without power as travel bans extend across the New York metro area
articles.body:
A powerful winter storm tearing up the U.S. East Coast has forced sweeping travel bans across the New York City tri-state area, grounding more than 10,000 flights and leaving thousands of residents without electricity as of Sunday, February 23.

Authorities in New York, New Jersey, and Connecticut extended travel restrictions as the storm intensified, effectively shutting down surface transportation across one of the nation's most densely populated corridors. The bans, which cover roadways in and around the New York metro area, reflect the severity of conditions that have made travel dangerous or impossible for millions of commuters and residents.

The aviation disruptions have rippled far beyond the region. With more than 10,000 flight cancellations reported nationwide, major hub airports including John F. Kennedy International, LaGuardia, and Newark Liberty International bore the brunt of the groundings. Airlines scrambled to rebook passengers, though full schedule recovery could take days given the scale of the disruption. The cancellations represent one of the largest single-storm flight impacts in recent years.

Power outages compounded the misery for residents across the Eastern Seaboard. Utility crews faced hazardous conditions as they attempted to restore service, with downed trees and heavy precipitation hampering repair efforts. The full scope of the outages remained unclear as the storm continued to move through the region, though officials warned that restoration could take an extended period in the hardest-hit areas.

The economic toll of the storm is expected to be significant. The New York metropolitan area, which generates roughly $2 trillion in annual GDP, saw commercial activity grind to a near halt as businesses closed and supply chains stalled. Insurers and emergency management agencies are bracing for a substantial damage assessment once conditions improve. Federal and state emergency declarations may follow depending on the storm's ultimate impact, which forecasters said would not fully abate until early in the coming week.

articles.headline: BitMine's Ethereum Bet Deepens as Unrealized Losses Surpass $8 Billion
articles.subheadline: Tom Lee's firm now holds 3.66% of all ether in circulation after another $98 million purchase amid falling prices
articles.body:
BitMine Immersion Technologies continued its aggressive accumulation of ether last week, purchasing 51,162 tokens worth approximately $98 million even as the cryptocurrency slid further and the company's total unrealized losses on its Ethereum treasury swelled past $8 billion.

The publicly traded firm, chaired by Fundstrat chief investment officer Thomas Lee, now holds more than 4.42 million ETH — roughly 3.66 percent of the token's entire circulating supply — making it by far the largest corporate holder of Ethereum. BitMine has spent an estimated $16.4 billion assembling the position, but with ether trading near $1,918 as of Sunday — down approximately 3 percent over the prior 24 hours — the portfolio is worth barely half that amount, according to data from DropsTab. Shares of BMNR have cratered roughly 60 percent over the past six months.

The strategy mirrors that of Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy), which pioneered the corporate bitcoin treasury model and recently logged its 100th bitcoin purchase announcement. But BitMine's Ethereum-focused approach carries distinct characteristics: the firm stakes more than 3 million of its tokens, generating what it says is $171 million in annualized staking revenue. That yield partially offsets the punishing mark-to-market losses, though it covers only a fraction of the drawdown. Beyond its crypto holdings, BitMine maintains 193 bitcoin, $691 million in cash, a $200 million equity stake in Beast Industries, and a smaller position in Eightco Holdings.

Lee framed the purchases as disciplined execution rather than capitulation. "In the midst of this 'mini crypto winter,' our focus continues to be on methodically executing our treasury strategy and steadily acquiring ETH and in turn, optimizing the yield on our ETH holdings," he said in the company's weekly update on Monday. The characterization of current conditions as a mini winter rather than a full-blown downturn is notable given that bitcoin has retreated to approximately $66,000 and Bitcoin ETFs have suffered $3.8 billion in outflows over a historic five-week streak.

The scale of BitMine's position raises concentration risk questions that extend beyond the company itself. Controlling nearly 4 percent of ether's total supply means any forced liquidation — whether driven by margin calls, shareholder pressure, or a need to shore up the balance sheet — could materially impact the token's price. For now, Lee appears intent on buying the dip. Whether the strategy ultimately vindicates or punishes shareholders may depend on whether the broader crypto market finds a floor before BitMine's losses grow any larger.

articles.headline: Private Equity Drought Surpasses 2008 Crisis as Unsold Assets Hit $3.8T
articles.subheadline: Bain report reveals industry returned fewer profits to investors for a fourth consecutive year amid deepening exit logjam
articles.body:
The private equity industry is now enduring a longer and more severe distribution drought than it experienced during the 2008 global financial crisis, according to a new report from Bain & Company, as firms sit on a record $3.8 trillion in unsold assets with no clear path to returning capital to their investors.

The consultancy's findings, published this week, paint a stark picture of an industry caught in a protracted liquidity squeeze. For the fourth consecutive year, private equity firms returned fewer profits to their limited partners — the pension funds, endowments, and sovereign wealth funds that provide the bulk of investment capital. The persistent shortfall has strained relationships between fund managers and their backers, making it increasingly difficult for firms to raise fresh capital for new investment vehicles.

The $3.8 trillion in unrealized assets represents a massive backlog of companies that private equity firms acquired during the cheap-money era but have been unable to sell at acceptable valuations. Rising interest rates, volatile public markets, and a sluggish dealmaking environment have conspired to keep exit activity depressed well beyond what most industry participants anticipated. Unlike the 2008 crisis — which produced a sharp but relatively brief disruption to deal flow — the current malaise has ground on for years, eroding confidence across the ecosystem.

The fundraising implications are significant. Limited partners who have not received expected distributions from existing funds have less capital — and less appetite — to commit to new ones. This dynamic has created a two-tier market in which the largest, most established firms continue to attract commitments while mid-sized and smaller managers face existential fundraising challenges. Some firms have turned to secondary markets, continuation vehicles, and net asset value lending facilities to manufacture liquidity, but these mechanisms have drawn scrutiny from investors wary of financial engineering that may obscure underlying performance problems.

The Bain report underscores a fundamental reckoning for an industry that grew rapidly during more than a decade of historically low interest rates. With approximately $3.8 trillion in assets awaiting exits, the pressure on private equity firms to find creative solutions — or accept lower returns — is unlikely to abate anytime soon. For the institutional investors that depend on private equity distributions to fund obligations ranging from pension payments to university budgets, the prolonged drought carries consequences that extend far beyond Wall Street.

articles.headline: Trump Threatens Netflix With 'Consequences' Over Board Member Susan Rice
articles.subheadline: The president's warning comes as Netflix's massive Warner Bros. acquisition awaits federal regulatory approval
articles.body:
President Donald Trump escalated his confrontation with corporate America on Saturday, warning Netflix it will "pay the consequences" unless it removes former Obama administration official Susan Rice from its board of directors — a threat that carries particular weight as the streaming giant awaits regulatory approval for its blockbuster Warner Bros. acquisition.

Trump's broadside, posted on Truth Social, came in response to comments Rice made on the "Stay Tuned with Preet" podcast hosted by former federal prosecutor Preet Bharara. Rice, who served as U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations and held senior advisory roles in both the Obama and Biden administrations, warned that corporations capitulating to the current administration would face a reckoning when Democrats return to power. "If these corporations think that the Democrats, when they come back in power, are going to play by the old rules… I think they've got another thing coming," Rice said on the podcast.

Trump responded by calling Rice a "racist, Trump Deranged" political operative and demanding her immediate termination from the Netflix board, where she has served since 2018. His post included a screenshot from far-right activist Laura Loomer, who alleged that the Netflix-Warner Bros. merger would create a "streaming monopoly" benefiting the Obamas through their Higher Ground production deal with Netflix. While Trump did not specify what consequences Netflix might face, the implicit leverage is considerable: the company's pending Warner Bros. acquisition requires sign-off from federal regulators. Netflix co-CEO Ted Sarandos reportedly met with Trump before the deal was announced, after which the president acknowledged Netflix as a "great company" but noted the transaction involves "a lot of market share."

The episode fits a pattern of Trump using presidential rhetoric — and the implicit threat of regulatory power — to pressure major corporations over personnel decisions. Last fall, Trump issued a similar demand that Microsoft fire its president of global affairs, Lisa Monaco, who was also singled out by Loomer. Monaco remains in her role at Microsoft. The question now is whether Netflix, with significantly more at stake given its pending mega-deal, will respond differently.

Neither Netflix nor Rice have publicly commented on Trump's post. The incident underscores the increasingly fraught relationship between the tech industry and the White House, where companies navigating major regulatory milestones face heightened vulnerability to political pressure campaigns targeting individual executives and board members.

articles.headline: Massive Winter Storm Paralyzes New York City, Grounds Thousands of Flights
articles.subheadline: Blizzard conditions threaten 41 million people across the U.S. East Coast as travel grinds to a halt
articles.body:
A powerful winter storm has effectively isolated New York City, grounding thousands of flights and disrupting travel across the eastern seaboard as approximately 41 million people brace for blizzard conditions.

The storm, which struck the U.S. East Coast on Sunday, brought punishing snowfall and high winds that forced major airports in the New York metropolitan area to halt operations. Thousands of flight cancellations rippled through the national aviation system, stranding travelers and cascading delays well beyond the immediate storm zone. Ground transportation in and around the city was similarly crippled, with road closures and hazardous driving conditions effectively cutting off the nation's largest city from its surroundings.

The National Weather Service had issued blizzard warnings covering a broad swath of the East Coast, with the storm expected to deliver heavy accumulations of snow, dangerously low visibility, and wind gusts strong enough to cause power outages and structural damage. Emergency management officials in New York and neighboring states urged residents to stay off roads and shelter in place as conditions deteriorated throughout the day.

The economic toll of the storm is expected to be significant. New York City, home to major financial institutions, corporate headquarters, and one of the world's busiest airspaces, faces disruptions that could extend into the workweek depending on how quickly cleanup operations proceed. Retailers, restaurants, and service-sector businesses across the affected region stand to lose revenue during what was shaping up to be a busy late-February period. Airlines, already contending with a challenging winter season, face mounting costs from cancellations, rebookings, and idle aircraft.

The storm represents one of the most disruptive winter weather events to hit the northeastern United States this season, underscoring the region's ongoing vulnerability to severe weather despite extensive infrastructure investments. City and state officials said they had pre-positioned plowing equipment and emergency personnel ahead of the storm's arrival, but acknowledged that the scale of the system would test response capabilities across multiple jurisdictions.

articles.headline: Stablecoin Growth Could Drive $1 Trillion in New T-Bill Demand by 2028
articles.subheadline: Standard Chartered says surging reserves from crypto-backed tokens may reshape U.S. debt issuance and suppress long-term yields
articles.body:
The rapid expansion of stablecoins could generate up to $1 trillion in fresh demand for U.S. Treasury bills by the end of 2028, potentially prompting the government to overhaul its debt issuance strategy and even suspend 30-year bond auctions, according to a new report from Standard Chartered.

The bank's analysts — Geoff Kendrick, head of digital asset research, and U.S. rates strategist John Davies — project the stablecoin market will grow from its current capitalization of roughly $300–$320 billion to $2 trillion by late 2028. Because issuers like Tether and Circle are required to back their tokens with liquid reserves, much of that growth will flow directly into short-dated government debt. Tether alone already holds T-bill positions rivaling those of mid-sized sovereign investors, while Circle parks a significant portion of its reserves in short-dated Treasuries through money market funds.

The implications for U.S. government financing are substantial. Standard Chartered estimates that stablecoin-driven demand, combined with $1–$1.2 trillion in projected Federal Reserve purchases, could push total new T-bill demand to approximately $2.2 trillion through 2028. With net new supply on track for only about $1.3 trillion if the current composition of debt issuance holds, that leaves a potential shortfall of roughly $900 billion. The Treasury itself flagged the trend in its February 4 Quarterly Refunding Announcement, noting it is actively monitoring growing private-sector appetite for bills.

The analysts argue this demand gap gives Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent room to increase the share of bills in total issuance by approximately 2.5 percentage points over three years, generating around $900 billion in additional short-term supply. That reallocation could come at the expense of longer-dated bonds — potentially suspending 30-year auctions entirely for three years — which would relieve upward pressure on long-term yields. While not its base case, the bank expects the 10-year yield to reach 4.6% by the end of 2026 and warned of rising risks of scarcity at the front end of the curve.

The outlook is not without headwinds. Stablecoin growth has stalled in recent months as crypto markets weakened, with Bitcoin falling more than 50% from its $126,000 peak in October 2025 to around $66,000. Post-GENIUS Act issuance has also slowed. But Standard Chartered views these pressures as cyclical rather than structural, maintaining that the crypto-to-Treasuries pipeline will reshape rate markets over the medium term. If the bank's projections hold, stablecoins — once a niche corner of digital finance — could become one of the most consequential sources of demand in the world's largest sovereign debt market.

articles.headline: Strategy Hits 100th Bitcoin Buy as Unrealized Losses Swell to $7 Billion
articles.subheadline: Michael Saylor's firm now holds 717,722 BTC worth roughly $47.5 billion — far below its $54.56 billion cost basis
articles.body:
Strategy, the software-company-turned-bitcoin-treasury-vehicle led by Executive Chairman Michael Saylor, marked a dubious milestone on Sunday: its 100th announced bitcoin purchase since it began accumulating the cryptocurrency in August 2020. The latest acquisition — 592 coins bought for $39.8 million at an average price of $67,286 each — was among the company's smallest, funded entirely through common stock sales.

The milestone arrives at an uncomfortable moment. With bitcoin trading near $66,200, Strategy's aggregate holdings of 717,722 BTC, acquired for a cumulative $54.56 billion at an average cost of $76,020 per coin, now carry an unrealized loss of approximately $7 billion. Shares of the Tysons, Virginia-based company, which trades on Nasdaq under the ticker MSTR, were down 2.5% in pre-market trading Sunday and have lost more than half their value over the past year.

Saylor, who has become the most prominent corporate evangelist for bitcoin as a treasury reserve asset, acknowledged the 100th purchase in a characteristically upbeat post on X. But the celebratory tone belied a strategy under mounting pressure. Bitcoin has struggled to regain momentum amid renewed U.S. tariff threats — including a proposed 15% global levy from President Donald Trump — and escalating geopolitical tensions between Washington and Tehran. The cryptocurrency briefly plunged to $64,270 overnight before stabilizing, while institutional outflows from bitcoin ETFs have totaled $3.8 billion over a historic five-week streak.

Strategy's bitcoin accumulation campaign, which began when the company was still known as MicroStrategy, has effectively transformed the firm into a leveraged bet on the digital asset. The company has relied on a combination of equity issuances, convertible debt offerings, and operating cash flow to fund purchases that now dwarf its original enterprise software business in scale. At current prices, the bitcoin hoard is worth roughly $47.5 billion — still an enormous position, but one that has slipped meaningfully underwater.

The broader crypto market reflected the strain on Sunday, with altcoins bearing the brunt of thin-liquidity selling. Solana and SUI fell 7% to 8%, contributing to $270 million in altcoin liquidations, though losses moderated during European trading hours. For Strategy and its shareholders, the question is no longer whether the company is committed to bitcoin — 100 purchases in under six years answers that definitively — but whether the market will reward that conviction or continue to punish it.

articles.headline: Killing of Jalisco Cartel Leader in Military Raid Triggers Violence Across Mexico
articles.subheadline: Clashes erupt in western Mexico following the death of a senior figure in one of the country's most powerful drug trafficking organizations
articles.body:
The death of a senior leader of the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG) during a security operation in the western state of Jalisco has sparked a wave of violent clashes across the region, according to reports from Bloomberg.

The raid, which resulted in the killing of the high-ranking cartel figure, prompted immediate retaliation from cartel operatives. Violent confrontations broke out in multiple locations in western Mexico, raising concerns about broader instability in a region long plagued by organized crime. Authorities have not yet disclosed the full details of the operation or the identity of the slain leader.

The Jalisco New Generation Cartel has risen over the past decade to become one of Mexico's most powerful and violent criminal organizations, with operations spanning drug trafficking, extortion, and territorial control across large swaths of the country. The group has been responsible for some of the most brazen attacks on Mexican security forces in recent years, and the killing of a top figure is likely to trigger a period of heightened volatility as rival factions and competing cartels seek to exploit any resulting power vacuum.

The clashes underscore the persistent security challenges facing Mexico's government, which has struggled to contain cartel violence despite periodic high-profile operations targeting criminal leadership. Analysts have long warned that so-called "kingpin strategy" operations — which focus on eliminating top cartel figures — can paradoxically increase violence in the short term by fragmenting criminal organizations and igniting succession struggles.

The unrest also carries economic implications for the Jalisco region, one of Mexico's most important economic corridors. The state is home to Guadalajara, the country's second-largest metropolitan area and a major hub for manufacturing, technology, and agriculture. Extended instability could disrupt supply chains and deter investment at a time when Mexico is positioning itself as a key beneficiary of nearshoring trends. Authorities are expected to increase security deployments in the area as the situation develops.
